questions.question: What is the atomic unit of a knowledge graph?
questions.explanation: KGs store information as (s, r, o) triples; aggregated triples form the graph.

questions.question: Why must each triple carry source provenance (doc id + span)?
options: Provenance lets you audit triples and reject hallucinations whose spans do not match the source text
questions.explanation: Provenance enables auditing and is the core of AEVS-style hallucination detection.

questions.question: What does canonicalization of relations do?
options: Maps surface verb phrases (e.g. 'was born in', 'is a native of') onto a fixed property id so the graph is queryable
questions.explanation: Canonicalization collapses paraphrases into canonical KG property ids.

questions.question: Why does relation extraction usually need coreference resolution first?
options: Pronouns like 'he founded Apple' must be resolved to a named entity before triple extraction
questions.explanation: Without coref, RE attaches relations to pronouns instead of the underlying named entity.

questions.question: Which choice trades open IE recall for graph queryability?
options: Mapping open-IE relations onto a closed ontology (e.g. Wikidata properties) before merging into the KG
questions.explanation: Closed ontologies make the graph queryable; the canonicalization step pays for itself downstream.

questions.question: Why do many production KGs use temporal qualifiers (start/end time)?
options: Many relations are time-bounded (employer, spouse, role); qualifiers prevent 'forever true' claims that go stale
questions.explanation: Time-bounded relations need qualifiers (e.g. Wikidata P580/P582) or facts go silently stale.
